metadata.name: A World Unknown
metadata.description:
Ua Pou is a majestic paradise located in the heart of the Pacific Ocean. With 2,213 inhabitants, Ua Pou holds a rich cultural history with traces of tribal wars and cannibalism. Four basalt pillars stand in the center of the island, creating a dramatic landscape few have seen. Stumbling upon this island as I looked for unique and unexplored territories to photograph, I knew I had to make the trip out there. Four flights later, with the final one being a small seaplane filled with only a few passengers, I arrived on Ua Pou full of excitement. I was quickly taken away by the lush green forests and the lack of infrastructure. I knew this destination would soon become of my favorites. I spent the next several days exploring the island and learning about the local culture. With such a scarce population, the locals shared many eye-opening stories about the current state of Ua Pou. Do you want to get your teeth checked out? A dentist visits the island every six months to attend to dental needs. Every person I stumbled upon was willing to share their family history and how this island went from tribal wars to living in harmony. Several locals were happy to drive me around and take me into their homes, generously sharing cuisines passed down for generations. The delicious spread of fresh-caught fish and french fries made from breadfruit gave me plenty of energy for every experience. While asking the locals for their favorite hikes, I  learned the territory was not well-traveled, and most adventures started with bush-whacking and a little creativity. That only added fuel to my exploration, and I traveled with locals keeping an eye on every turn. On a gloomy drive through the jungle, I spotted a rocky ridgeline through the cracks of overgrown trees and brush. I quickly asked my local guide to stop so I could take a closer look. The ridgeline was located approximately two miles from the gravel road we were on and was guarded by thick brush and crumbling rocks. I asked the guide if there were any hikes or trails that may get us to the edge of the ridge. He quickly answered, "No, but that doesn't mean we can't get there. We can always give it our best shot!" The local guide proceeded to grab his machete and started paving the way through the shrubs. The hike was nothing short of exhausting, and I quickly realized how intense of a trek this would be. Crumbling cliff-sides followed the endless cuts from the sharp shrubbery. Finally, after a long grueling hour soaked from the humidity, we had made it onto the small mountain. Every one of us on the trek stared in awe of the beauty we discovered. We stood atop the crest gazing at the large pillars piercing in and out of the clouds. As soon as I sent my drone up, I knew the hard work and determination had paid off. I stood in complete shock next to my girlfriend as I snapped a photo of us on the peak. I knew this would be a memory I would always hold so close. Even the local shouted in joy, stunned by the view of his island. As we were getting ready to pack up, the local asked me, "What would you like this hike to be named?" To this day, my mind is blank. All I can think about is the extraordinary journey. As for a name, I guess I'll have to let the collector decide :) 
Video Description : https://youtu.be/2Xucov0zZPY

It lives outside distraction, routine and keeping up with the neighbors. It envelopes you like love and sunshine at the beach. The gateway is imagination and perhaps it’s not so far away. I’ll be heading there soon, would you like to join me?

Behind the scenes:
This was a dream shoot to accomplish. It took 3 different trips visiting and meeting all sorts of people who had links to the owners to get permission to shoot, and we almost got told no the day of. Luckily we were able to get the right person to say yes despite being told a government council needed 6 months to approve; we drove 8 hours and woke up a dance company at 5am to get in position for the sunrise light. I still can’t believe we got the shot in such an iconic location I’d been dreaming of for years.

metadata.name: A New Light
metadata.description:
Storms are a necessity in nature and in life.  They can be unpredictable and uncomfortable but once you make it through, clarity ensues. “A New Light” represents weathering the storms and inviting in new chapters.
This is an image that was taken in the remote Canadian wilderness on what started out as a typical sunny summer day.  A hail storm ensued on a ridge where the only shelter available was in the mind.  After weathering the storm , this perfect pocket of light came out for just a few minutes.  With no one around, it was an incredibly intimate moment that I am privileged to share on the blockchain.  I knew in my heart I wouldn’t leave this place until I was happy with an image because it was  a long drive and very long hike to do.  This fleeting moment of meditation and calm in the harsh mountains felt curiously meant to be and I knew it was the one instantly.

metadata.name: The Crossing
metadata.description:
When I created this garden scene, I poured my soul into every shape and form. This isn't merely a landscape—it's a reflection of the quiet sanctuary I've always sought within myself.
The crimson arc of the bridge rising gracefully over azure waters represents my own crossing between worlds—from chaos to peace, from noise to silence. I've stood on such curved pathways many times, feeling suspended between what was and what could be.
maple trees, with their star-shaped leaves capturing light in emerald and amber hues that still surprise me, remind me of the family who sheltered me through seasons of change. I rendered them in that magical moment between viridian summer and russet autumn—the fleeting palette that breaks my heart open year after year, teaching me that impermanence itself is what makes life precious.
I named this piece "ザ・クロッシング" because every meaningful journey in my life has felt like crossing a bridge, leaving something behind while moving toward something unknown. The stone path invites you to walk alongside me, to pause where I've paused, to breathe where I've breathed.
This garden doesn't exist in the physical world, yet it is more real to me than many places I've stood. It lives in that floating world between memory and dream—ukiyo—where I often find myself when brush meets canvas and heart meets truth.

metadata.name: The path of the hero
metadata.description:
In this piece, the artist seeks to share a space for reflection regarding various symbolic elements that accompanied the history of the human being. The recognized image of the ascending serpent refers to the kundalini energy that travels through our spine and releases the energy blockages that lie in different parts of the body. Likewise, the animal represents knowledge and wisdom. The balance that this work presents speaks of the balance and the strength that is gained by crossing and advancing along the path of life. Shedding our skin like the snake does, and learning to let go but without forgetting our history, going through difficulties without losing the origin, building a path of light and consciousness in complete connection with the divinity that we are.
Every day of life I stop for a second to see my hands and I am surprised by the depth that lies behind everything that is presented to us habitually before our eyes. I seek surprise, I seek not to take anything for granted and to see clearly that everything that surrounds me and everything that will surround me will always be magic.

I want to especially thank Manuela Hidalgo for having walked with me for several years, for having lived one of the most beautiful love experiences in life, and because today our bond continues to be created through art.
Thank you for the contribution of photographic material for the construction of this piece

For the last four years, Ryan has explored and photographed hundreds of glaciers and ice caves, making it his mission to tell the story of the ice, creating visually stunning pieces that inspire both curiosity and awe of our world.

metadata.name: Stasis
metadata.description:
I am from Nebraska. I had lived in Nebraska for 24 years of my life. To say that life outside of cornfields and flatland would even be possible for me seemed a bit farfetched. But I wanted to try. In 2014, I sold all of my possessions and booked a one way ticket to Japan. I was going to walk across Japan...and after 5 grueling months, I did it. 4 years later, I would wind up traveling across the globe to Iceland after befriending a man that I met along my journey across Japan in the years prior. He had become a glacier guide with his wife and I joined them. For 3 years I trained amongst the ice, learning it's story and photographing it. Today, me and the man own a glacier tour company together.

I now trek across the frozen landscapes for a living and teach travelers about the ice and how it shapes our world. 

This photograph is not one that was taken on the glacier. It is much more unique than that. This photograph was taken on a frozen glacier lake inside of an iceberg. As a glacier reaches lower altitudes, the ice calves and forms massive icebergs in the lakes. In the wintertime, it gets so cold that these lakes freeze and the icebergs become locked in a stasis until summertime. There is a brief window where teams of glacier guides can trek across the lakes and explore these ice caves formed inside of the icebergs. This ice cave was formed at the roof of the biggest iceberg on the lake and in order to explore it, we had to repel down into it. 

As I photographed my friend rappelling into the static iceberg on a frozen lake in the middle of Iceland, it dawned on me how absolutely insane my life had become. 

6 years prior, I took a step out my door without understanding where it would lead me, and it lead me here.

To this static moment in time.

metadata.name: VIII. STRENGTH
metadata.description:
Upright: Strength to do difficult things gently, wisdom, confidence in tough choices, protector, coming of age.

Reversed: Not knowing one’s own power, capriciousness, finding the easy way out, killing the inner child too soon.

A woman sits in a prairie of goldenrod just hidden from the view of a neighborhood behind her. She is barefoot and casually dressed, gently engulfing a fawn laying between her legs. Looking to her right, she holds a silver dagger to the throat of the fawn. On her right arm is an infinity tattoo. 

When the violence of a hot midwestern summer begins to cool, the goldenrod flower blooms everywhere, filling the prairies and roadsides with a final ode to the sun. The goldenrod traditionally symbolizes encouragement and growth, but also caution. The woman here is suspended in this moment between seasons, a period I like to think of as  "summer's grace." The summer heat brings incredible forces of life that clash and burn, growth rapidly overlaps, plants fighting for the sun strangle one another, creatures reproduce and most will not survive through the season, for the abundance of life also means the strength of predators.  In this moment, the peak of summer has past and the infinite cycle turns toward the mercy of a cooling atmosphere, towards an inevitable autumn, towards the grace of death.

metadata.name: The Kiss
metadata.description: This artwork was created in May of 2024. This piece is a nod to and inspired by Pablo Picasso’s 1969 artwork. It was his fascination with with raw, almost primitive forms, marked by bold outlines and exaggerated features that inspired this piece. The artwork serves as a study and explores the same abstract style with heavy, simplified lines and a focus on form over realism. Apart from the abstracted and simplified forms, this work was created as my first exploration of digital painting and creating controlled chaos as I painted intentionally while also allowing my hand to create the unexpected and continue working from what was often unintentionally formed. It was an exciting first attempt to navigate a fully digital painted piece.

metadata.name: WAKE UP
metadata.description: I have this recurring nightmare. It starts with me in a crowded city where no one acknowledges my existence. I am fully aware that I'm dreaming and my nightmare is now beginning. The idea of waking up terrified sends me into a panic. I start to run aimlessly hoping to fully wake up. In my panic I try to take deep breaths but can't draw air. Desperately trying to cry out for help I can hear the inaudible murmurs gasping for air in the real world. When I awaken I take control of my breathing as my body sits paralyzed, unable to move. When this dream occurs my mind is giving me a warning that I am losing hope. It is time to reevaluate what I am doing and find a new hope that works better for me

metadata.name: The Deathless Lives Again
metadata.description:
Cold eyes hiding cunningly behind an aegis of gloom. Daring glare, scornful like the devil’s snare whispering — “what is dead is never truly dead.” Your savage hands reek of the raw scent of woe, angst and everything in between.  You’re not a man. You’re not quite the beast. Are you the thing that makes life seem too short to live? Or you’re the truth attests to the phoenix’s myth?
 
And like a ball of yarn, I lay loosely in your hands. You push, I pull. Silent night but your poise echoes loudly through the corners of the room. Wrapped in a semblance of indifference but I know what’s coming. I hear the words that your mouth isn’t saying —“Alas! The deathless lives again.”

metadata.name: Skeleton Key
metadata.description: I realized quickly after I began my teenage years that people did not think like I did. Not in a way that gave me an advantage or disadvantage, but in a way that was a catalyst for me using my perspective to explore creative outlets. There was one universal language for me and it was art. I was not always able to express in words what I was dealing with but if I sat down with paint or a pencil I could use what I was interested in to represent it even if only for myself. A teacher in high school insisted I talk more about this, he was my history teacher and I was failing his class. He told me he knew I was smart but that someone along the way had convinced me I couldn’t hold my own. Art was the skeleton key that unlocked countless doors in my life, and those conversations with him helped me find that key. So the man in this piece represents him.

metadata.name: Tempest
metadata.description:
There comes a time in everyone's life where an unexpected road will lead us on a beautiful journey. For me, this journey began on remote County Road 4 in Nebraska in 2018. 

I'll never forget stepping out of the car and feeling the muggy summer heat against my skin. As the wind hurriedly blew across the corn laden landscape, I stepped into the road and laid eyes upon this multi-layered supercell. Rising at over 50,000 feet, I gazed in awe at the sheer beauty of Mother Nature as this storm churned and twisted in the sky towards me. It was at this moment I felt as though a mere speck of sand upon this vast sphere of dust and water called Earth. 

"Tempest" was the first structured supercell I ever saw and kickstarted an unexpected passionate journey into storm chasing and storm photography. Most roads are meant to carry us from point A to point B. The road and storm you're staring at in this piece has taken me on a road trip where the destination is unknown, but the journey itself is filled with the rawest connections to Mother Nature.

metadata.name: TIME "The Man of the Year" - January 2nd, 1928
metadata.description:
*** THIS NFT WILL BE ACCOMPANIED WITH THE ORIGINAL, PHYSICAL ISSUE. *** 

On January 2nd, 1928, TIME named Charles Lindbergh its first "Man of the Year," establishing one of the most acknowledged global recognitions to this day. 

In 1999, TIME "Man of the Year" gave way to "Person of the Year." And, in 2020, TIME established the "100 Women of the Year" project to spotlight the influential women who were often overshadowed. 

This is the genesis of this recognition. Charles Lindbergh. 1927 Man of the Year.

From the issue:

"To date [Lindbergh] has flown to France; Belgium; England; Mexico; Canada in the interests (his) of aviation progress and the interests (governmental) of international good will ... Grumblers wondered if interest accruing to the national welfare by his flights is worth the calamitous crash of principal which would accompany his death. Col. Lindbergh is the most cherished citizen since Theodore Roosevelt. Thought they: 'He is worth keeping.' One way to keep him is to keep him on the ground.

Others argued savagely that Lindbergh must fly for his life in the public eye; heroes age swiftly when seated at office desks; argued that by his very nature he must fly.

Unconscious of these wrangles over the national coffee cups, Col. Lindbergh tended to business. He climbed into The Spirit of St. Louis at Mexico City; nosed upward; set off for Guatemala, British Honduras, Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panama.

In Detroit a school teacher put by her pointer and her students' papers. Mrs. Evangeline Lodge Lindbergh dressed herself warmly and was swept southward by the propeller windstorm of a sturdy trimotored Ford monoplane. One night she spent in St. Louis. The next day as her famed offspring in Mexico City was piloting on his first flight President Plutarco Elias Calles, the monoplane sprang to Tulsa, Okla. The third sunset found her in Brownsville, Texas. Next day up from the crowded field at Mexico City rose Col. Lindbergh in The Spirit of St. Louis. Swallowed in the clouds he missed the monoplane which he had flown to meet. Shouts from the field of 'Vivi Senora Leenbaire' as Mrs. Lindbergh stepped out of the Ford plane. She met her wandering boy an hour later at the American Embassy.

En route Mrs. Lindbergh was loquacious. Previously laconic regarding the achievements of her amazing child she expressed herself to the press thus:

'He has always been my boy. I have always loved him, been proud of him and thought he was the world's greatest.'"

metadata.name: Study for a Portrait (for Francis)
metadata.description: I find Francis Bacon creeping into my work sometimes, partly titled after one of his paintings. This work is part of my series “Color, Balance” where bodily artifacts removed from fashion photography become the base for paintings I create in Photoshop. I add subtle layers of digital paint and push pixels to blend the figure’s remnants into the background. I use color as a spatial feature, shifting away the boundary of the body to further blur lines between abstraction and figuration. An aspect of my practice engages the use of computer software that removes all traces of my hand— the process iterates the disappearance of the physical body on and off the screen. With this work, I aim to express the complexity of 21st-century identity, and the disconnect between the body and those around us. Physical works from this series where shown at my solo exhibition “I Am A Portrait” at Undercurrent, Brooklyn in January 2020.

metadata.name: The Activation
metadata.description: In the year 2487, earth does not resemble the green utopia we once knew. Long uninhabited, only whispers of what it once was remain. Through the ancient ruins, sculptures and artifacts left by human ancestors, they speak to us of a power long lost. One that transforms worlds through creation and destruction. They told us of our fate and pointed to the stars. So we followed to try and save what remained. Light years across the blanket of space in search of an answer, we finally return. Setting this key in its rightful place, the device set in stone, rises, spins, and ignites with a glowing green light. From the center of the statue a river of green flows. Flowers bloom, soil enriched, and sprouting trees cascade the ruins of the temple before us. A fog of moisture rolls in rising the grass under our feet. A fountain of life undoing the damage done. Within time, this oasis should spread to the rest of the world. We stand here in awe of the activation of new life.

This is why telling the story of the world means so much to me, you never know the next time you might be able to witness a never to be seen again moment. This is a moment to remind you to take a deep breath and take a look around at your surroundings. A moment frozen in time.

metadata.name: Exhale
metadata.description:
Have you ever wondered why everyone seems to love sunsets and sunrises?  Or why wide, sweeping vistas capture our imagination more than narrow, enclosed views?  
  
My own theory about this can be summed up in one word:  Safety.  
  
Forget old sailors' aphorisms; if you're in a place in the world where the sky glows pink and red and orange, you can breathe a sigh of relief.  The same goes for having miles of visibility; unknown threats can't sneak up on you here, and you've got a better chance at suriviving any dangers that *do* roll your way.  
  
These feelings of security transfer over to our artistic and visual preferences, leading to quiet moments spent sipping coffee in the morning light, or to grueling hikes to reach the best possible vantage point.  We all seek safety in our own ways.  
  
---  
  
This morning scene at Preikestolein in Norway is one of my favorite memories from that trip.  We woke up an hour early and hiked up to the bluffs above the rock to get the best view of the campers in their tents below taking in the sunrise.  In accordance with the themes of the caption above, this sunrise shot is one of the widest-angle images I've ever minted as an NFT.  
  
---

**Series:**  Non-Fungible Travels

metadata.description: In late 2013 I was stuck in a toxic and mentally destructive work environment and I built a dirty 3D kitchen to cope with that. In the following three years, I couldn't create anything original. This was the first image after that hiatus and probably the most known thing I've ever made. Today, it's the 'face' of Octane Renderer - users around the world see it everyday and it inspired hundrets of artist to start their career in that field. The whole piece took around 3 to 4 months to complete and the rendertime for one highres frame was around 50 hours.
